6 min read
Just now

--

By Via Lee｜VyntaHQ · The Dev Log Published: July 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Designer’s Real Friction Points (Image generated with AI.)
This story was written with the assistance of an AI writing program.
After we published #01 — and finished hands-on testing Claude Design’s new Design System feature — the team’s main focus for the past few weeks has been usability. During internal testing, we ran into a handful of friction points and engineering hurdles in the workflow — Yoha has already resolved them.
This round centered on two things:

• Filling in the guidance. We went back and added every prompt that non-engineers found unfamiliar or easy to miss on a first run, to lower the cognitive load of getting started.

• Making the process reversible. Earlier versions of the flow felt like a one-way street at certain points in the conversation. Yoha rebuilt it so that when you’re running /generate-component and want to tweak an earlier choice, you can step back to it directly, which cuts down on the need to redo everything after a wrong pick.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The updated README now walks through each slash command — /setup-design-guideline, /sync-tokens, /generate-component—with example prompts, filling in the guidance gaps non-engineers ran into.This round, product designer Nina ran the full test, working entirely by dropping in screenshots. As someone without much of an engineering background, the friction she ran into is much closer to what Vibe Kit’s actual target users will experience.
Even With the README, She Still Got Stuck
Looking at Nina’s feedback, one thing stood out: even when she followed the README step by step, she still had to spend a lot of time asking the AI basic engineering questions just to keep moving — things like “what’s the difference between Clone and Fork,” or “what do I do after I’ve set up this environment?”
One thing to flag upfront: this is my own read, not a measured number — Nina wasn’t tracking tokens as she worked, so whether this path actually hit our token-saving goal is still an open question. What it did give us was something else worth having: a much clearer picture of how designers actually think their way through the flow, and where that diverged from what Yoha had originally pictured.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Two-Phase Testing Flow (Image generated with AI.)
Core Architecture & Testing Flow
The test ran in two main phases: environment setup, and defining the design system through conversation with the AI.
Step 1–4: Environment Setup & Project Download (Where Most of the Friction Lives)

• The expected path: Fork → Clone → npm install → open in VS Code.

• What actually happened: Nina mixed up Fork and Clone, assuming forking the repo meant it was already cloned.
Then she hit a dead end in the terminal over GitHub credentials and permissions. She eventually gave up on terminal commands entirely, working around the problem with GitHub Desktop and opening the project directly in VS Code.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The README’s environment setup steps: opening the project in VS Code, launching Claude Code, and cloning the forked repo.Press enter or click to view image in full size

Fork ≠ Clone: Where the Expected Path Broke Down (Image generated with AI.)Step 5–10: Defining Design Specs With Claude Code

• How it worked: using the /setup-design-guideline command, Claude runs a Q&A to define brand colors, neutral colors, typography, and dark mode—the basic building blocks of any component.

• What stood out: instead of handing over the JSON spec an engineer would normally write, Nina just dropped in screenshots and worked through the rest in conversation with Claude:

• Brand colors: she uploaded a screenshot, and Claude pulled three hex codes from it, suggesting on-brand names like sand, blush, or lavender.

• Neutrals: she uploaded a second screenshot, and Claude identified four base hex codes, then asked whether she wanted to expand them into a 10-shade palette.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Nina uploaded a brand color screenshot, prompting the AI to extract three hex codes and suggest on-brand names like sand, blush, or lavender.Press enter or click to view image in full size

Nina uploaded a new screenshot to redefine neutrals, prompting the AI to identify four base hex codes and ask if she wanted to expand them into a 10-shade palette.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Four Pain Points, Four Fixes (Image generated with AI.)
Core Pain Points & Engineering Fixes
The Wall of Git Commands

• What designers ran into: terminal password prompts don’t show any characters at all, for security reasons — which is exactly where Nina got stuck before she’d even started.

• Our fix: we took the command out of the equation. The Clone step is now just a prompt pasted into Claude Code, and Claude handles the download itself. Going forward, users never have to touch Git or the terminal.
Permission Requests

• What designers ran into: when Claude asked for terminal permission to run npm install, Nina didn't know what npm even was. Combined with a few setup issues, the environment never fully came together, and Storybook never ran.

• Our fix: we folded the step into the flow. The README now leads with a safety note explaining what npm install does and why Claude needs permission to run it, and the command itself is bundled directly into the /start flow—Claude decides when to run it and handles the whole thing. Storybook now gets scaffolded automatically as part of that same environment setup, so there's no separate step where it can silently fail to launch.
A Redundant Comma

• What designers ran into: a stray trailing comma in package.json tripped up npm before it could even get started.

• Our fix: Yoha fixed the syntax error by hand. (During testing, the AI actually caught the error and fixed it on the spot.)
Reversibility Nobody Knew Existed

• What designers ran into: partway through the process, Nina didn’t realize she could go back and change colors she’d already locked in earlier.

• Our fix: we made Claude raise it first. When the flow wraps up, or when Claude detects an existing config file, it now resurfaces the existing values and asks, “What would you like to change?” — making it explicit, in the moment, that earlier choices can always be revisited.

Core Conclusions & Key Insights
This round of testing left us with three takeaways worth sharing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Three Takeaways (Image generated with AI.)
• Environment friction is one of the real barriers keeping Vibe Coding out of non-engineers’ hands
The moment a product touches terminal setup, permissions, or Git commands, it drops a designer into an unfamiliar world. Deciding which steps to quietly automate inside Claude Code, and which ones still need to be spelled out in the README, will take more rounds of user testing to find the right balance.

• Claude Code can actually solve problems when things break
Because the environment wasn’t fully set up, Storybook never ran during this test — a classic error path. Even so, Claude found a way through: when Nina asked, “Can I preview this?” it generated an HTML file on the spot so she could see the colors directly in her browser.

• A feature existing isn’t the same as a user knowing it exists
Vibe Kit already supports going back and changing earlier choices, and already guides people through multiple-choice prompts. None of that matters if the interface and the conversation never say so out loud — without an explicit “you can always change this later,” users will default to assuming it’s a one-way street.
That’s why the system leans on frequent, conversational Q&A to close the gap the interface alone can’t: at one point, the AI guided Nina through an interactive Q&A to confirm her font choices before smoothly moving on to the dark mode question.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When Storybook failed, Nina asked for a preview, prompting the AI to dynamically generate an HTML file to display the colors in her browser instead.Press enter or click to view image in full size

The AI guided Nina through an interactive Q&A to confirm her font choices before smoothly moving on to the dark mode question.
What’s Next
We’ve already gone through this round’s results with Yoha and made some changes — including a more intuitive way for designers to see their own design system and component library at a glance.
For round three, I’ll test the newly optimized and added features myself first, before bringing Nina back in. We’ll cover what we find in the next Dev Log.
If you haven’t read our first Dev Log introducing Vibe Kit, you can check it out here.
